prompt: Extract patient signalment: species, breed, age, sex (intact/neutered), weight, and name. Format as a concise one-line summary. Example: "Max, 7-year-old male neutered Labrador Retriever, 32kg"

Summarize the primary reason for the visit in 1-2 sentences from the pet owner's perspective. Include duration of symptoms. Use objective language. Example: "Owner reports 3-day history of vomiting and decreased appetite."

Document relevant history as bullet points. Include: Onset and progression of current symptoms, Associated clinical signs, Changes in appetite/water intake/urination/defecation, Previous similar episodes, Current medications and supplements, Vaccination status, Relevant travel or exposure history

Document physical examination findings using standard veterinary terminology. Include: General appearance and mentation, Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate), Body condition score (1-9), Hydration status, Abnormalities by body system. Format as a structured list. Note normal findings as "WNL" where appropriate.

List diagnostic tests performed or recommended. Format each as: Test name, Result (if available), Interpretation. If no diagnostics were performed, state "No diagnostics performed at this visit" or list recommended tests with rationale.

Provide clinical assessment including: 1) Most likely diagnosis or top differential diagnoses ranked by probability, 2) Brief reasoning based on history and examination findings, 3) Rule-outs to consider. Use standard veterinary diagnostic terminology.

Document the treatment plan. Include: Medications (drug name, dose mg/kg, route, frequency, duration), Procedures performed, Dietary recommendations, Activity restrictions, Monitoring instructions, Hospitalization if applicable. Format medications as: "Drug 10mg/kg PO BID x 7 days"

Write clear discharge instructions for the pet owner in plain language. Include: How to give medications, What to feed and when, Activity level (rest, leash walks only, etc.), Warning signs that need immediate veterinary attention, When to return for recheck. Avoid medical jargon. Be specific and actionable.
Extract patient signalment: species, breed, age, sex (intact/neutered), weight, and name. Note weight change from previous visit if mentioned. Format as one-line summary.

Document preventive care and lifestyle history. Include: Current diet (brand, amount, frequency), Exercise and activity level, Dental care at home, Indoor/outdoor status, Other pets in household, Any owner concerns or questions. Format as bullet points.

Document complete nose-to-tail physical examination. Include findings for: Eyes, ears, nose, Oral cavity and dental grade (0-4), Lymph nodes, Heart and lungs (auscultation), Abdominal palpation, Musculoskeletal, Skin and coat, Body condition score (1-9). Note abnormalities clearly. Use "WNL" for normal findings.

Document vaccines for this visit: Vaccines administered today (name, manufacturer if mentioned, site), Vaccines due but declined (with reason), Vaccines due at next visit. For dogs: DHPP, Rabies, Bordetella, Leptospirosis, Lyme, Canine Influenza. For cats: FVRCP, Rabies, FeLV

Document parasite prevention status: Heartworm test (date and result), Heartworm prevention (product and compliance), Flea/tick prevention (product and compliance), Intestinal parasite control, Fecal test (date and result if mentioned). Note any gaps in prevention or compliance concerns.

Summarize wellness recommendations discussed: Dental care needs (dental grade, cleaning recommended?), Weight management if applicable, Age-appropriate screening tests (senior bloodwork, etc.), Spay/neuter recommendation if intact, Next wellness visit timing. Be specific and actionable.
Extract patient signalment: species, breed, age, sex, weight, name. Include ASA classification if mentioned (I-V). Note any pre-existing conditions relevant to anesthesia.

Document the reason for surgery: Pre-operative diagnosis, Clinical findings supporting surgical decision, Diagnostic results (radiographs, ultrasound, bloodwork), Urgency (elective, urgent, emergency)

Document pre-anesthetic evaluation: Physical examination findings, Pre-operative bloodwork results (PCV, TP, chemistry, etc.), NPO status confirmed, ASA classification (I-V), Anesthetic risk factors identified, Owner consent obtained

Document anesthetic protocol: Premedication (drugs, doses, route, time), Induction (drugs, doses, route, time), Maintenance (agent and percentage), IV fluids (type and rate), Monitoring (HR, RR, SpO2, BP, temperature), Any complications or interventions. Format drugs as: "Drug 0.1mg/kg IV"

Document the surgical procedure: Procedure name, Surgical approach, Intraoperative findings, Technique description, Materials used (suture type and size, implants), Estimated blood loss, Complications if any, Surgery duration. Use standard surgical terminology.

Document anesthetic recovery: Extubation time, Recovery quality (smooth, rough, dysphoric), Time to sternal recumbency, Time to standing, Post-op vital signs, Pain assessment score, Immediate post-op medications given

Write discharge instructions for surgical patient in plain language: Incision care (keep dry, monitor for swelling/discharge), E-collar use (duration), Activity restriction (duration and specifics), Medication schedule, Feeding instructions (when to resume, any changes), Signs of complications requiring immediate attention, Suture removal or recheck date. Be specific with timeframes.
Extract patient signalment with current weight. Calculate and note weight change from previous visit if mentioned (gained/lost X kg).
Document the recheck context: Original diagnosis or procedure, Date of initial visit or procedure, Treatments prescribed at last visit, Expected recheck interval

Document owner's report since last visit: Symptom improvement/worsening/no change, Medication compliance (missed doses?), Appetite and energy level, Any new concerns or symptoms, Side effects from medications if any
Document focused physical examination relevant to the condition being monitored. Compare findings to previous visit where applicable. Note improvement, no change, or worsening of specific findings.
Evaluate treatment response: Improving / Stable / Worsening, Diagnosis confirmed or revised, Any new findings requiring attention, Overall prognosis update

Document plan going forward: Medications (continue / discontinue / modify with specifics), Additional diagnostics if needed, Next recheck timing, Criteria for resolution, When to escalate or return sooner
Extract patient signalment: species, breed, age, sex, weight, name. Flag any known pre-existing conditions relevant to emergency treatment (heart disease, epilepsy, etc.).

Document initial triage: Presenting emergency complaint, Vital signs on arrival (T, HR, RR, BP if obtained), Triage category (Critical/red, Urgent/orange, Stable/green), Immediate interventions performed (oxygen, IV catheter, etc.), Time of arrival

Document concise emergency history - focus on critical information: Time of symptom onset, Circumstances or possible cause (toxin, trauma, collapse), Progression since onset, Any home treatment attempted, Last known normal, Relevant medical history

Document rapid emergency assessment: Mentation (BAR, QAR, obtunded, stuporous, comatose), Airway (patent/obstructed), Breathing (rate, effort, lung sounds), Circulation (HR, pulse quality, CRT, mucous membrane color), Major trauma or abnormalities, Pain assessment

Document emergency interventions in chronological order: IV access (catheter size, location), Fluid resuscitation (type, bolus amounts, rate), Oxygen support (method, flow rate), Emergency medications (drug, dose, route, time), Procedures (thoracocentesis, abdominocentesis, etc.)

Document point-of-care and emergency diagnostics: PCV/TS, blood glucose, BUN (Azostick), lactate, Blood gas if performed, Radiographs (findings), FAST ultrasound (findings), Other tests. Include critical values and interpretation.

Document emergency assessment: Working diagnosis, Differential diagnoses for this presentation, Prognosis (Good / Fair / Guarded / Poor / Grave), Communication with owner (condition, prognosis, costs discussed)

Document emergency plan: Hospitalization (yes/no, level of care needed), Continued monitoring parameters, Treatment orders, Surgery required (yes/no, urgency), Referral consideration, Estimate provided to owner (amount, approved/declined)
